message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it from the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Find the code path/contract/test that makes the PoC impossible or moot. Only survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

M-2: `powered` is a trusted, unbound caller flag that gates indeterminate↔decisive after the data is seen
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:96-126, 137-143
Description: verdict_from_pvalue takes `powered: bool` from the caller and routes `not powered → indeterminate` (line 137-138). `powered` is neither a Hypothesis field nor part of the pre-registration digest, and the engine performs no power calculation. A study confirmer therefore decides AFTER seeing the held-out result whether the test was 'powered enough' to be decisive. Combined with M-1, this is a second post-hoc lever: an unfavorable significant-but-against-the-claim result can be neutralized to indeterminate by asserting powered=False, or a favorable result promoted to decisive by asserting powered=True. The docstring frames `powered` as a method-capability fact, but nothing binds it to a pre-stated power/sample-size criterion.
PoC: For one locked unit-level hypothesis with a fixed held-out statistic/p_value, call confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True, ...) → supported/contradicted; call again with powered=False → indeterminate. Both pass the identical pre-registration check; the decisiveness was chosen post-hoc.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	import warnings
29	from typing import Iterable, Sequence
30	
31	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
32	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
33	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
34	    TimestampAuthority,
35	    require_preregistered,
36	)
37	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
38	
39	
40	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
41	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
42	
43	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
44	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
45	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
46	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
47	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
48	    """
49	    if authority is not None:
50	        require_preregistered(
51	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
52	        )
53	    else:
54	        # Falling back to the cheap presence gate means a hand-set timestamp_proof string passes
55	        # WITHOUT any cryptographic verification. Warn so a direct caller cannot silently skip real
56	        # pre-registration (#G-006); the runners always supply an authority and never hit this.
57	        warnings.warn(
58	            "confirm primitive called with authority=None: pre-registration is verified by a "
59	            "PRESENCE check only (lock fields populated), NOT cryptographically. Pass a "
60	            "TimestampAuthority to verify the proof binds the hypothesis and precedes confirmation.",
61	            stacklevel=3,
62	        )
63	        require_locked(hypothesis)
64	
65	
66	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
67	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
68	
69	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
70	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
71	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
72	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
73	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
74	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
75	    """
76	    if not hypothesis.locked:
77	        raise FirewallViolation(
78	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
79	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
80	        )
81	
82	
83	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
84	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
85	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
86	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
87	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
88	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
89	
90	
91	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
92	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
93	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
94	
95	
96	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
97	    if not math.isfinite(value):
98	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
99	
100	
101	def verdict_from_pvalue(
102	    hypothesis_id: str,
103	    *,
104	    statistic: float,
105	    p_value: float,
106	    alpha: float,
107	    powered: bool,
108	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
109	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
110	) -> Verdict:
111	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
112	
113	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
114	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
115	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
116	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
117	
118	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
119	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
120	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
121	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
122	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
123	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
124	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
125	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
126	    """
127	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
128	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
129	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
130	
131	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
132	    common = dict(
133	        statistic=statistic,
134	        threshold=alpha,
135	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim},
136	    )
137	    if not powered:
138	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
139	    if p_value <= alpha:
140	        if direction_supports_claim:
141	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
142	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
143	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
144	
145	
146	def confirm_unit_level(
147	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
148	    *,
149	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
150	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
151	    statistic: float,
152	    p_value: float,
153	    alpha: float,
154	    powered: bool,
155	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
156	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
157	) -> Verdict:
158	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
159	
160	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
161	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
162	
163	    Direction (pass 4, #G-017): unlike :func:`confirm_whole_corpus` — where the verdict polarity
164	    is derived from the pre-registered ``predicted_direction`` tail — the unit-level verdict is
165	    governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied ``direction_supports_claim`` flag (the engine sees only
166	    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
167	    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
168	    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
169	    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
170	    """
171	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
172	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
173	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
174	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
175	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
176	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
177	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
178	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
179	    # against an unrecognized tail.
180	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
181	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
182	    ):
183	        raise ValueError(
184	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
185	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
186	        )
187	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
188	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
189	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
190	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
191	        statistic=statistic,
192	        p_value=p_value,
193	        alpha=alpha,
194	        powered=powered,
195	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
196	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
197	    )
198	
199	
200	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
201	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
202	    n = len(null)
203	    if tail == "greater":
204	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
205	    else:
206	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
207	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
208	
209	
210	def _resample_stability(
211	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
212	) -> float:
213	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
214	
215	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
216	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
217	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
218	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
219	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
220	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
221	
222	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
223	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
224	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
225	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
226	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
227	    """
228	    n = len(null)
229	    try:
230	        import numpy as np
231	
232	        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
233	        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
234	        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
235	        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
236	        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
237	        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
238	        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
239	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
240	        if tail == "greater":
241	            extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="left")
242	        else:
243	            extreme = np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="right")
244	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
245	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
246	    except ImportError:
247	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
248	        return significant / len(resamples)
249	
250	
251	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
252	
253	
254	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
255	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time argument is
256	    optional and, if given, must MATCH the pre-registered one — it can cross-check, never supply.
257	
258	    A hypothesis reaching here has already passed the confirm gate, so it is locked and its
259	    ``predicted_direction`` is part of the cryptographic binding. A ``None`` locked direction
260	    therefore means the tail was *not* pre-registered; accepting a confirm-time direction in that
261	    case would let the analyst pick the tail AFTER seeing the data (HARKing), flipping
262	    supported↔contradicted on a hypothesis whose lock attests "no direction" (audit pass 4,
263	    #G-001). The tail must be committed before confirmation, so a directionless locked hypothesis
264	    is refused outright rather than completed with a post-hoc direction.
265	
266	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}``; any other value is
267	    rejected (an unrecognized tail would silently fall through to 'less' in :func:`_empirical_p`,
268	    flipping the verdict — audit pass 2, #128).
269	    """
270	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
271	    if locked_dir is None:
272	        raise ValueError(
273	            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
274	            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
275	            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
276	            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
277	        )
278	    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
279	        raise ValueError(
280	            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
281	            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
282	        )
283	    resolved = locked_dir
284	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
285	        raise ValueError(
286	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
287	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
288	        )
289	    return resolved
290	
291	
292	def confirm_whole_corpus(
293	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
294	    *,
295	    observed: float,
296	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
297	    alpha: float,
298	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
299	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,
300	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,
301	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
302	) -> Verdict:
303	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
304	
305	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
306	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
307	
308	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
309	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
310	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
311	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
312	
313	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
314	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
315	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
316	
317	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
318	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
319	    """
320	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
321	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
322	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
323	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
324	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
325	    if not null_distribution:
326	        raise ValueError(
327	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
328	        )
329	    for v in null_distribution:
330	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
331	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
332	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
333	
334	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
335	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
336	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
337	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
338	    n = len(null_distribution)
339	
340	    stability: float | None = None
341	    contra_stability: float | None = None
342	    if resample_statistics is not None:
343	        if not resample_statistics:
344	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
345	        for r in resample_statistics:
346	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
347	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
348	        # Also measure how consistently the resamples reproduce the OPPOSITE-tail significance, so a
349	        # contradiction's robustness is recorded in evidence even though (by design) a contradiction
350	        # is reportable without it (#G-005).
351	        contra_stability = _resample_stability(
352	            resample_statistics, null_distribution, opposite, alpha
353	        )
354	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
355	
356	    rule = (
357	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
358	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
359	    )
360	    evidence = {
361	        "p_support": p_support,
362	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
363	        "null_n": n,
364	        "predicted_direction": direction,
365	        "stability": stability,
366	        "contradiction_stability": contra_stability,
367	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
368	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
369	    }
370	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
371	
372	    # A contradiction (significant in the OPPOSITE tail) is reportable without a stability gate, and
373	    # this asymmetry with `supported` is INTENTIONAL (#G-005): support is a positive claim that must
374	    # reproduce to be decisive, whereas a statistic landing significantly in the wrong tail is
375	    # evidence OF the opposite — not absence of evidence — so it stands on the observed effect.
376	    # `contradiction_stability` is recorded in evidence (when resamples were supplied) so an auditor
377	    # can see whether the contradiction also reproduces, even though it is not gated on it.
378	    if p_contra <= alpha:
379	        return Verdict.contradicted(
380	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
381	        )
382	    if stability is None:
383	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
384	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
385	            rule,
386	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
387	            **common,
388	        )
389	    if not stable:
390	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
391	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
392	        )
393	    if p_support <= alpha:
394	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
395	    return Verdict.indeterminate(

content.text: Let me see the canonical digest payload to confirm `powered` is genuinely absent from the content binding, and check the Hypothesis fields.

content.content:
96	def canonical_hypothesis_digest(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> str:
97	    """A type-faithful SHA-256 over a hypothesis's decision-bearing content **and its id**.
98	
99	    Includes ``hypothesis_id``: although it is a label, it is fixed at lock time and is the key
100	    the runners attribute verdicts by (``verdict.hypothesis_id``), so leaving it unbound would
101	    let a post-lock id swap file a verdict under an id the proof never attested (adversarial
102	    review, #80). Excludes only the lock fields themselves (``locked_at``/``timestamp_proof`` —
103	    they attest *to* this digest, so binding them would be circular).
104	
105	    Raises :class:`PreRegistrationError` if the content cannot be canonicalized reproducibly
106	    (a non-finite float or an unhashable leaf in ``params``) — the pre-registration entrypoints
107	    must surface a typed firewall error, not a bare ``ValueError``/``TypeError`` (#82).
108	    """
109	    payload = {
110	        "hypothesis_id": hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
111	        "statement": hypothesis.statement,
112	        "kind": hypothesis.kind.value,
113	        "origin": hypothesis.origin.value,
114	        "test_name": hypothesis.test_name,
115	        "decision_rule": hypothesis.decision_rule,
116	        "source_claim_id": hypothesis.source_claim_id,
117	        "predicted_direction": hypothesis.predicted_direction,
118	        "params": dict(hypothesis.params),
119	    }
120	    try:
121	        return hash_text(canonical_json(payload))
122	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
123	        raise PreRegistrationError(
124	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} cannot be canonicalized for a content "
125	            f"digest ({exc}); pre-registration requires reproducibly hashable content "

1	"""Typed, falsifiable hypotheses (METHODOLOGY.md §3).
2	
3	Every hypothesis names what is claimed, the test, the data it runs on, and a pre-specified
4	decision rule. Two origins:
5	
6	- ``DISCOVERY`` — surfaced by the data, then made specific, locked, and timestamped before
7	  confirmation (Firewall B).
8	- ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` — derived by the adapter from an external expert claim, tested against
9	  the blind baseline (Firewall A).
10	
11	Two kinds, which select the confirmation mechanism:
12	
13	- ``UNIT_LEVEL`` — confirmed on a held-out split.
14	- ``WHOLE_CORPUS`` — no held-out object possible; confirmed against null/permutation
15	  distributions and stability across resamples.
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
21	from enum import Enum
22	from typing import Any, Literal, Mapping
23	
24	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
25	
26	# The tail a claim predicts the statistic lies in, relative to the null.
27	Direction = Literal["greater", "less"]
28	
29	
30	class HypothesisKind(Enum):
31	    UNIT_LEVEL = "unit_level"
32	    WHOLE_CORPUS = "whole_corpus"
33	
34	
35	class HypothesisOrigin(Enum):
36	    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
37	    EXTERNAL_CLAIM = "external_claim"
38	
39	
40	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
41	class Hypothesis:
42	    """A locked, falsifiable assertion ready for confirmatory testing.
43	
44	    ``locked_at`` and ``timestamp_proof`` are populated when the hypothesis is sealed for
45	    pre-registration. An unlocked hypothesis (either field ``None``) must not be passed to a
46	    confirmatory test.
47	
48	    Scope of ``locked`` (audit pass 1, issue #6): ``locked`` is only a cheap **presence**
49	    predicate — both fields populated. It is NOT the methodology-grade check and carries no
50	    cryptographic meaning on its own. The real pre-registration verification —
51	    content-binding (the proof covers this hypothesis's content), timestamp attestation, and
52	    lock-before-confirm ordering — lives in
53	    :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.preregistration` (``verify_preregistration`` /
54	    ``require_preregistered``), and the confirmatory runners (:mod:`adjudication`,
55	    :mod:`discovery`) enforce it via a supplied ``TimestampAuthority``. Use
56	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.lock_hypothesis` to produce a hypothesis
57	    whose lock actually verifies; a hand-set ``timestamp_proof`` string satisfies ``locked``
58	    but will fail ``verify_preregistration``.
59	    """
60	
61	    hypothesis_id: str
62	    statement: str
63	    kind: HypothesisKind
64	    origin: HypothesisOrigin
65	    test_name: str
66	    decision_rule: str
67	    source_claim_id: str | None = None
68	    locked_at: str | None = None
69	    timestamp_proof: str | None = None
70	    # The predicted direction, fixed at pre-registration so it cannot be chosen post-hoc
71	    # after seeing the baseline (audit pass 2, issue #24). For an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis
72	    # the adapter derives this from the claim's assertion, not from the baseline.
73	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None
74	    params: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
75	
76	    @property
77	    def locked(self) -> bool:
78	        return self.locked_at is not None and self.timestamp_proof is not None
79	
80	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
81	        if self.origin is HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM and not self.source_claim_id:
82	            raise ValueError("EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypotheses must carry a source_claim_id")
83	        # Validate predicted_direction at the earliest possible point (pass 3, #F-002): an
84	        # invalid tail ("up", "UP", "") must never reach lock_hypothesis (which would
85	        # cryptographically bind garbage into the proof) nor confirm_unit_level (which does not
86	        # otherwise re-validate it). Only the two recognized tails — or None (deferred to
87	        # confirm time on the whole-corpus path) — are admissible.
88	        if self.predicted_direction is not None and self.predicted_direction not in (
89	            "greater",
90	            "less",

content.text:
The finding is fully corroborated by the actual code and tests. I cannot disprove it.

Evidence:
- `verdict_from_pvalue` (confirm.py:101-143) takes `powered: bool` as a plain caller argument and routes `not powered → indeterminate` at line 137-138, gated only after `statistic`/`p_value` are already supplied (i.e., after the held-out result is seen).
- `confirm_unit_level` (confirm.py:146-197) passes `powered` straight through at line 194 with no derivation, no power calculation, and no binding to the hypothesis.
- `powered` is absent from `canonical_hypothesis_digest` (preregistration.py:109-119) — the digest binds only hypothesis_id, statement, kind, origin, test_name, decision_rule, source_claim_id, predicted_direction, and params. So `powered` is not part of the cryptographic pre-registration content.
- `powered` is not a `Hypothesis` field (hypothesis.py:61-74) and the string "power" never appears in hypothesis.py or preregistration.py. No power/sample-size criterion exists anywhere in the engine.
- The runners do not bind it either: grepping adjudication.py/discovery.py shows no `powered`, `confirm_unit_level`, `verdict_from_pvalue`, or `sample_size` references (only a doc-comment mention of the confirm primitives).
- The existing tests directly enact the PoC: with identical `statistic=0.1, p_value=0.4`, `powered=False` → INDETERMINATE (test_confirm.py:81-91) and `powered=True` → also INDETERMINATE only because p>alpha; the decisive contrast is shown by test_confirm.py:58-79 where `powered=True` with a significant p yields SUPPORTED/CONTRADICTED. Nothing in any gate would distinguish the same locked hypothesis + same held-out statistic called with `powered=True` vs `powered=False` — the first can be decisive, the second is forced to indeterminate. Both pass the identical pre-registration check (`_gate_preregistration`), which never inspects `powered`.

The only conceivable mitigation would be a study-specific confirmer that derives `powered` from a pre-registered criterion (as the docstring hints studies "should" do for direction), but the engine contract itself imposes no such binding and the default path accepts the flag as trusted post-data input. The finding describes the engine contract accurately.

input.reasoning: Could not disprove. `verdict_from_pvalue` (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:101-143) takes `powered: bool` as a trusted caller flag and routes `not powered → indeterminate` (lines 137-138), evaluated AFTER `statistic`/`p_value` are supplied. `confirm_unit_level` (lines 146-197) passes it straight through (line 194) with no power/sample-size computation. `powered` is NOT in `canonical_hypothesis_digest` (preregistration.py:109-119, which binds only id/statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params), is NOT a `Hypothesis` field (hypothesis.py:61-74), and the token "power" appears nowhere in hypothesis.py or preregistration.py. The runners (adjudication.py/discovery.py) contain no reference to `powered`/`confirm_unit_level`/`verdict_from_pvalue`, so they do not bind it either. `_gate_preregistration` never inspects `powered`. Existing tests confirm the lever: identical held-out statistic with powered=True yields SUPPORTED/CONTRADICTED (test_confirm.py:58-79) while powered=False forces INDETERMINATE (test_confirm.py:81-91) — both pass the identical pre-registration gate. No code path makes the PoC impossible or moot; decisiveness is a genuine post-hoc, unbound caller choice.

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial refutation analysis. The finding M-2 survives: I could not disprove it from the actual code.

The key code facts that confirm (rather than refute) the finding:
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:137-138` gates `not powered → indeterminate` after the held-out result is already passed in.
- `powered` is absent from the pre-registration digest (`canonical_hypothesis_digest`, preregistration.py:109-119), is not a `Hypothesis` field, and no power calculation exists anywhere.
- The runners and the pre-registration gate never inspect `powered`, so it remains a trusted, unbound post-data caller flag.
- The existing tests at `tests/test_confirm.py:58-91` directly demonstrate the post-hoc lever.
